--- SOURCE 1: Harnessing Solar Energy with Plasmonic Nanostructures ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/plasmonic-nanostructures-for-solar-energy

SUMMARY:
Plasmonic nanostructures have emerged as a promising technology for enhancing the efficiency of solar energy harvesting and conversion.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Photonic–Plasmonic Nanostructures for Solar Energy ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/

SUMMARY:
In this review, recent research progress in the development of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures for various applications in solar energy conversion, such as photovoltaics, photothermal conversion, and photocatalysis, is highlighted. Furthermore, the combination of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures for developing high-efficiency solar energy conversion systems is explored and discussed. Integrating photonic or plasmonic nanostructures with semiconductors has been proven as an alternative to improve the solar energy conversion efficiency [2]. Herein, the applications of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures in solar energy conversion and biosensing are reviewed. We discuss different approaches to enhance solar energy conversion efficiency by incorporating semiconductors with photonic, plasmonic, or hybridized photonic–plasmonic nanostructures. *   47.Linic S., Christopher P., Ingram D.B. Plasmonic-Metal Nanostructures for Efficient Conversion of Solar to Chemical Energy. Photonic–Plasmonic Nanostructures for Solar Energy Utilization and Emerging Biosensors
======================================================================================

[Van Tan Tran](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Tran%20VT%22%5BAuthor%5D)

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)

PMCID: PMC7696832 PMID: [33198391](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33198391/)

Abstract
--------

Issues related to global energy and environment as well as health crisis are currently some of the greatest challenges faced by humanity, which compel us to develop new pollution-free and sustainable energy sources, as well as next-generation biodiagnostic solutions. Optical functional nanostructures that manipulate and confine light on a nanometer scale have recently emerged as leading candidates for a wide range of applications in solar energy conversion and biosensing. In this review, recent research progress in the development of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures for various applications in solar energy conversion, such as photovoltaics, photothermal conversion, and photocatalysis, is highlighted. Furthermore, the combination of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures for developing high-efficiency solar energy conversion systems is explored and discussed. We also discuss recent applications of photonic–plasmonic-based biosensors in the rapid management of infectious diseases at point-of-care as well as terahertz biosensing and imaging for improving global health. Finally, we discuss the current challenges and future prospects associated with the existing solar energy conversion and biosensing systems.

**Keywords:** photonic–plasmonic, nanostructure, solar energy, light harvesting, biosensor, terahertz

1. Introduction
---------------

The application of solar energy in addressing global energy and environmental issues has recently attracted increasing attention, and various techniques for the same have emerged. Solar energy can be converted directly into electrical or chemical energy through photovoltaics or photoelectrochemical cells and photocatalysis, respectively, which is generally governed by four processes: light absorption, charge separation, charge migration, and charge recombination. However, as individual semiconductor materials cannot be optimized for all four processes, the overall efficiency of solar conversion is very low, hindering their practical applications. There are two main reasons for the low efficiency of solar energy conversion: limited light absorption and high recombination rates of charge carriers. The first reason is large bandgaps of the most commonly used photocatalysts such as TiO 2 and ZnO, which is the main challenge in their solar energy conversion applications. The second reason for the low efficiency of solar energy conversion is the high electron–hole recombination rate in the bulk of a semiconductor owing to the short diffusion length of photoexcited minority charge carriers.

Several approaches have been proposed to overcome the limitations of the existing semiconductors. For example, ultraviolet (UV) bandgap semiconductors were doped to increase the absorption of visible light. However, this approach leads to high charge recombination rates due to the detrimental charge mobility caused by isolated midgap states [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B1-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Sensitizers are also used to convert absorbed visible-light radiation to electricity and transfer the photoexcited charge carriers to semiconductor support. Organic dyes or quantum dots are generally used in sensitizers; however, both are plagued by photostability issues, particularly when used in photocatalysis or photoelectrochemical cells. Integrating photonic or plasmonic nanostructures with semiconductors has been proven as an alternative to improve the solar energy conversion efficiency [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B2-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. The resonant scattering near photonic bandgap and slow-photon effects of photonic crystals (PCs) can effectively improve interactions between semiconductors and light. The enhancement of light absorption in the semiconductor induced by plasmonic nanostructures is attributed to their ability to increase the path length of light and concentrating the incident electromagnetic field. Further, the energy transformed from the metal to the semiconductor can induce charger separation, reducing the electron–hole recombination rate in the semiconductor.

The current health crisis caused by infectious diseases also compels us to develop next-generation diagnostics. Photonic- and plasmonic-based platforms have been proven to be the key candidates for biomolecular research and biomedical applications. Owing to their unique structural and optical features, PC-based nanostructures have been proven as an important diagnostic tool to effectively control infectious diseases. Biosensing using PCs is mostly based on changes in the refractive index due to the molecules or pathogens that are in the vicinity of the PCs. Furthermore, plasmonic nanostructures, which can generate intense and highly localized electromagnetic (EM) fields, have been demonstrated to be extremely sensitive transducers to track small local refractive index changes caused by the adsorption of biomolecules to their surface [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B3-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. A variety of diagnostic methods have emerged based on photonic, plasmonic nanostructures, including colorimetric assays, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), surface plasmon polariton (SPP), optical waveguide, and terahertz sensing and imaging.

Herein, the applications of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures in solar energy conversion and biosensing are reviewed. For solar energy conversion, three main applications are reviewed: photovoltaic, photothermal, and photocatalytic applications. We discuss different approaches to enhance solar energy conversion efficiency by incorporating semiconductors with photonic, plasmonic, or hybridized photonic–plasmonic nanostructures. Furthermore, the mechanism of various sensing modalities, such as photonic, plasmonic, and plasmonic–photonic colorimetric assays, and recent advancements in biosensing strategies to achieve clinically relevant performances within the POC feasible frameworks will be discussed. Finally, we provide an overview of the challenges and prospects of the current research studies on the photonic–plasmonic-based solar energy conversion and emerging biosensors.

2. Photovoltaics
----------------

### 2.1. Photonic Crystal-based Broadband Antireflective Surfaces

Photovoltaic (PV) devices that convert solar energy into electrical power are among the most researched devices for solar energy conversion. Enhancing light absorption is one of the key factors determining the overall conversion efficiency, especially in thin-film solar cells. To improve light trapping, it is important to understand the types of nanostructures and their optimal spatial arrangement. For example, by manipulating the structure of photonic nanostructures, the light–matter interaction can be enhanced via increasing the optical path length within the absorbing medium and their interaction times, resulting in enhanced optical absorption [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B4-nanomaterials-10-02248),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B5-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. The high reflection loss at the glass–air interface, which degrades the performance of solar cells, can be reduced by an antireflective (AR) coating. However, a narrow AR bandwidth and small incidence angle range of conventional AR coatings restrain the solar cell performance. An alternative approach to suppress reflection losses over a wide range of wavelengths has been widely introduced in various types of solar cells by producing a gradual reduction of refractive index away from the solar cell top surface. Based on this strategy of broadband reflection suppression, the performance of various PV devices, including perovskite and silicon solar cells [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B6-nanomaterials-10-02248),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B7-nanomaterials-10-02248)], germanium-based PV cells [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B8-nanomaterials-10-02248)], and gallium arsenide (GaAs)-based solar cells [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B9-nanomaterials-10-02248),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B10-nanomaterials-10-02248)], was significantly increased. The form of moth-eye antireflective schemes, which are also referred to as nanotapered arrays, is among the most effective approaches for effective AR over a wide range of useful solar spectra. A single-junction Ge/InGaP solar cell with a PC-nanostructured surface showing antireflective properties was one of the very first experimental applications utilizing this effect [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B11-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Kim et al. demonstrated nanostructured PDMS films inspired by moth-eyes fabricated using a soft lithography method, showing good structural fidelity and notably improved performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B12-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Highly efficient PSCs, with a power conversion efficiency exceeding 20%, were obtained with 300 nm periodic structures ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#nanomaterials-10-02248-f001)a–d). Similarly, Choi et al. reported that optimized AR surfaces are composed of moth-eye-patterned polyurethane-acrylate of 300 nm [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B13-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Baquedano et al. assessed the optical and surface properties of nanostructured antireflective solar glass, which saw an increase in transmission and wettability in 1D- and 2D-ordered nanostructures compared to those of disordered nanostructures [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B14-nanomaterials-10-02248)].

#### Figure 1.

[![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/e0e0/7696832/2ecd24fcebe1/nanomaterials-10-02248-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=7696832_nanomaterials-10-02248-g001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/figure/nanomaterials-10-02248-f001/)

Nanostructured moth-eye antireflective surface. Effective antireflective (AR) technique for the solar spectrum. (**a**) Focused ion-beam-assisted cross-sectional scanning electron microscopic images of a moth-eye PDMS film with a 300 nm periodicity. (**b**) Perovskite solar cell (PSC) configuration imaged in a back-scattered electron mode. (**c**) Transmittance spectra of the differently prepared samples of glass, bare PDMS, and moth-eye films with 300 and 1000 nm periodicities. (**d**) Digital camera image demonstrating the antireflective effect of the 300 nm moth-eye PDMS film. Diameter and height of the hexagonal array are equal to periodicity in all PDMS films. These figures have been reproduced with permission from [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B12-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Copyright Springer, 2019.

### 2.2. Photonic Crystal-based Omnidirectional Antireflective Surfaces

Light trapping from large angles of incidence is another strategy to enhance the conversion efficiency of a PV cell. Luo et al. developed SiO 2 nanosphere-based AR coatings using a facile, inexpensive, and scalable spin-coating technique. Because of the isotropic photonic structures of SiO 2 nanospheres, the optimized coating exhibits enhanced transmittance over a broadband of 400–800nm and less angular dependence for incident light, increasing the omnidirectional PV performance of solar cells [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B15-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Various photonic-based AR coatings inspired by insects and plants have also been developed to improve PV performance, particularly in enhancing the light-harvesting efficiency in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B16-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Yun et al. proposed a strategy to enhance the performance of DSSCs by designing an omnidirectional light-capturing layer that mimics structural epidermal cells in leaf structures. Light-harvesting layers with different shapes inspired by the epidermal structures of various plant species were fabricated and incorporated into DSSCs, resulting in enhanced capturing efficiency of obliquely incident light up to 70% [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B17-nanomaterials-10-02248)].

### 2.3. Mechanisms for Incorporating Plasmonic Nanostructures in Solar Cell Systems

Incorporating plasmonic nanostructures in solar cell systems is an alternative option for improving PV conversion efficiency [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B18-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Besides enhancing light–matter interactions, surface-plasmon-enhanced local electric fields can also effectively suppress the electron–hole recombination in semiconductors. The mechanisms of the plasmonic-enhanced solar cell include: (1) light trapping, (2) hot-electron injection, and (3) local electromagnetic field enhancement. Here, we briefly discuss these mechanisms.

#### 2.3.1. Light Trapping

Thin-film solar cells with microscale thickness have attracted considerable interest because of their unique advantages over the thicker counterparts, including an increased open-circuit voltage (_V_ OC) and cost/time-effective processes. However, one of the main issues of thin-film solar cells limiting their performance is the low absorption coefficient. Due to the unique scattering properties, large metallic nanoparticles (NPs) can increase the optical path length, leading to effective light trapping within the semiconductor that has a much smaller layer thickness compared to the material’s intrinsic absorption length. Positioning metallic NPs on the surface of a semiconductor can induce a forward directional scattering into the semiconductor. Light scattered at angles exceeding the critical angle of reflection can also be effectively trapped within the solar cell when equipped with a metallic reflector on its back. Therefore, the optical path length can be efficiently increased to trap incident light since it is concentrated into the semiconductor and travels multiple times through it. Using this strategy, by incorporating the solar cell with an Al NP array, the absorption rate and current density of thin-film GaAs solar cells were significantly enhanced up to 0.7983 and 25.77 mA/cm 2, respectively [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B19-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Other strategies using nanostructured plasmonic thin films and metallic grating structures were proposed to effectively enhance light coupling, trapping, and absorption in ultrathin solar cells [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B20-nanomaterials-10-02248),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B21-nanomaterials-10-02248),[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B22-nanomaterials-10-02248),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B23-nanomaterials-10-02248),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B24-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. An average absorption of 90% over a broadband of 400 to 900 nm along with near independence of light polarization and an incident angle over a range of 0°–75° was achieved using a thin plasmonic cavity consisting of a 30 nm thick Au metallic mesh electrode with a subwavelength hole-array [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B25-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. The impressive light-trapping enhancement of the plasmonic nanohole arrays was attributed to multiple cavity modes and surface plasmon modes in the structure.

#### 2.3.2. Hot-Electron Injection

This mechanism is based on the injection of plasmon-induced hot electrons with sufficient energy to overcome the Schottky barriers at the metal/semiconductor into the conduction band of the neighboring semiconductor [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B26-nanomaterials-10-02248),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B27-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Au nanorods (NRs) were used to generate the majority of charge carriers in a plasmonic PV device, which are energetic enough to clear the Schottky barrier or quantum-mechanically tunnel through it, thereby producing the output photocurrent [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B28-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Under short-circuit conditions, hot electrons with visible wavelength incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) values of ~1.0% and IQE of 2.75% were extracted, which are considerably higher than those previously reported for plasmonic photovoltaics under zero-bias conditions. Using Ag nanoplates integrated between Si nanowires (NWs) and PEDOT/PSS, Lui et al. proposed a concept to absorb and convert near-infrared (NIR) light into hot electrons. The hot electrons overcame the Schottky barrier between Si and Ag and flowed into the conduction band of the Si NWs. The external quantum efficiency of flexible NIR PV devices was improved by 59% using this strategy [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B29-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Park et al. recently developed a strategy to enhance the lifetime and flux of plasmon-nduced hot electrons, which are decisive for developing practical hot-carrier solar cells [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B30-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Nanodiodes consisting of a perovskite layer CH 3 NH 3 PbI 3 (MAPbI 3) were stacked on a plasmonic Au/TiO 2. They found that quantum efficiency and the short-circuit photocurrent were increased by the deposition of MAPbI 3 on both continuous thin-film Au and randomly connected Au nanostructures. They also confirmed that integrating MAPbI 3 with the Au nanostructure notably prolongs hot-electron lifetimes, which can be associated with the high photocurrent in nanodiode ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#nanomaterials-10-02248-f002)).

##### Figure 2.

[![Image 10: Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/e0e0/7696832/7e9b70fd4204/nanomaterials-10-02248-g002.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=7696832_nanomaterials-10-02248-g002.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/figure/nanomaterials-10-02248-f002/)

Generation of plasmonic hot electrons. (**a**) Schematic of the MAPbI 3/plasmonic Au/TiO 2 nanodiode (left) and magnified illustration of the active area (right). (**b**) Incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) as a function of incident photon energy. (**c**) Short-circuit photocurrents measured on the MAPbI 3/plasmonic Au/TiO 2 nanodiodes with respect to the number of MAPbI 3 layers. Schematic drawing of the hot-electron pathways for the MAPbI 3/thin-film Au/TiO 2 (**d**) and the MAPbI 3/plasmonic Au/TiO 2 (**e**) structure. (**f**) Theoretically drawn energy levels of the MAPbI 3/plasmonic Au/TiO 2 structure. Reproduced with permission from [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B30-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Copyright American Chemical Society, 2019.

#### 2.3.3. Local EM Field Enhancement

As per this mechanism, the strong enhancement of the local EM field induced by localized plasmon resonances of the metal nanoparticles can also promote the generation of photoexcited electrons and holes in solar cells [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B31-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. This strategy is usually implemented by embedding plasmonic nanostructures in the active layer, which act as optical antennas to store the incident light energy in the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), leading to effective enhancement of the light absorption in the solar cell. For example, Bayle et al. experimentally proved plasmonic near-field enhanced light absorption by embedding Ag NPs in organic metal halide perovskite thin-films [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B32-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Light-harvesting at the maximum resonance frequencies and over the entire spectral range was increased up to 80% and 20%, respectively. Au NRs capped with AgS 2 were integrated into the photoanode of DSCs to enhance longer wavelength sunlight utility, leading to an improvement in photocurrent by 37.6% in the spectral region of 600–720 nm due to the longitudinal plasmon resonance of Au NRs [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B33-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. Gold nanorods (Au NRs) and magnesium oxide were more recently introduced into the mesoporous perovskite solar cells, which resulted in the improvement of near-field and far-field solar energy conversion of over 15% [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B34-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. The embedded Au NRs enhanced PV performance by utilizing the longitudinal plasmon resonances (LPRs), which also support higher absorption and scattering cross-sectional efficiency, improving the EM field intensity significantly, especially at the wavelengths near the LPR peak (665 nm). In another study, Shen et al. reported plasmon-enhanced MAPbI 3 thin-film perovskite solar cells using nanopatterned plasmonic arrays [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B35-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. The calculated PCE of 45.5% was found in the nanopatterned plasmonic solar cell compared to its flat counterpart.

At present, the use of photonic and plasmonic nanostructures in PV devices has advantages and disadvantages. However, combining photonic crystal and plasmonic nanostructures with solar cell devices can induce synergetic light manipulating for resonant-enhanced light harvesting and power conversion. Li et al. proposed a coupling architecture of an Au NP-impregnated inverse opal TiO 2 film to enhance the visible and NIR light absorption of DSCs [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7696832/#B36-nanomaterials-10-02248)]. The light absorption and the overall light-to-electricity conversion yield were increased up to 62% and 41%, respectively, compared with the control test.

3. Photothermal Conversion
--------------------------

The Shockley–Queisser limit, which is caused by the radiative recombination and sub-bandgap transition losses of semiconductors, defines the maximum theoretical efficiency of a single-junction PV device. Although extensive studies have been conducted to overcome practical problems with PV cells, such as reflection and surface defects, solar–thermal conversion approaches may provide an alternative method for overcoming this theoretical limit and increasing the solar energy capture and conversion efficiency. The nonradiative decay process of the surface plasmons, once thought to be only a burden limiting plasmonic device perf

## Abstract

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

Solar thermal conversion technology, as an emerging solar energy harvesting strategy, has attracted much attention in many application areas. In this field, plasmonic materials have a strong application potential owing to their localized surface plasmon resonance. The use of plasmonic materials with extensive solar energy absorption, combined with their practical applications, provides new routes for realizing efficient photothermal conversion in multidisciplinary applications. In this review, we discuss the photothermal conversion of different nano/microstructures based on various plasmonic systems for hydrogen production, desalination, and bacterial inactivation applications, including the design of plasmonic systems coupled with a variety of materials for enhanced photocatalysis, localized heating, and salt resistance. Finally, new design concepts and current advances for scalable wastewater purification as well as bacterial and hydrogen detection are discussed. The aim of this review is to summarize the latest developments in the application of plasmonic materials in solar photothermal conversion technologies, as a way to stimulate the transition of fundamental research to practical industrial applications, facilitate the effective use of new energy sources, and provide new research strategies for the sustainable production of energy.

## Introduction

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

The rapid growth of industrialization results in an increasing depletion of fossil energy sources, greatly exacerbating the energy crisis and driving continuous attempts to develop new, clean, green, and renewable sources of energy to cope with the current shortage. [(1−3)](javascript:void(0);) Various types of clean energy sources have been developed in recent years, such as wind, tidal, geothermal, and solar energy; among them, solar energy attracts wide interest because of its abundant reserves and extensive irradiated area. [(4,5)](javascript:void(0);) The annual solar irradiation is approximately 3,400,000 EJ, [(6)](javascript:void(0);) which is much higher than the global energy loss, making sunlight one of the renewable energy sources considered as potential solution to the current energy crisis. Therefore, the efficient collection and conversion of solar energy for various applications has been widely studied in recent years.

Currently, there is great interest in the efficient collection and conversion of broad-spectrum sunlight through photothermal materials, and a variety of related application technologies have been developed. Many photothermal materials have been developed to perform efficient sunlight conversion, such as plasmonic materials, [(7−9)](javascript:void(0);) composites, [(6,10−12)](javascript:void(0);) carbon-based materials, [(13,14)](javascript:void(0);) and solar absorbers. [(15,16)](javascript:void(0);) Based on their localized plasmon resonance properties, plasmonic materials enable fast and efficient photothermal conversion in the broad spectral range of sunlight, endowing them with great application potential in several fields, including desalination, antimicrobial treatment, photocatalysis, and more. [(17)](javascript:void(0);) Although these technologies have undergone rapid research and development in recent years, there have been few comprehensive reviews integrating and reporting these advancements. More attention has been given to the photothermal conversion capabilities of plasmonic nanomaterials for solar energy. [(18−20)](javascript:void(0);) Therefore, a systematic review of recent developments in plasmonic materials is urgently needed.

Plasmonic materials exhibit unique optical and thermal properties, owing to their unique nano/microstructure. The inherent narrow-band absorption of plasmonic materials can be modified by photothermal coupling of nanoparticle microstructures, resulting in broadband absorption. The light absorption of plasmonic materials can be greatly enhanced by surface plasmon resonance coupling between neighboring particles of different shapes. [(21)](javascript:void(0);) Therefore, they are considered ideal materials for efficient photothermal applications. As a rising star among technologically mature and cost-effective photothermal materials, plasmonic materials show strong potential for applications in light-driven hydrogen production, solar-driven interfacial desalination, and wastewater purification. The rapid development of plasmonic materials in related frontier fields has enabled recent advances in many new structures, strategies, and innovative preparation techniques, leading to the next research hotspot and frontier. Therefore, informing the wider scientific community about key advances is a crucial task in this field.

In this review, we provide a detailed overview of current developments in plasmonic nanomaterials for cutting-edge research in multiple fields. We first focus on efficient green hydrogen production by plasmonic materials with different nano/microstructures. Moreover, nano/microstructure-enhanced water evaporation strategies for solar-driven interfacial desalination are discussed in detail. Then, plasmonic materials for wastewater purification and antimicrobial strategies are reviewed ([Figure 1](#fig1)). Finally, challenges and opportunities for the future development of plasmonic nanomaterials in the above applications are discussed. This review may provide important guidance to stimulate future research in this field.

## Plasmon-Enhanced Photocatalytic Hydrogen Production

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

With the increasing depletion of fossil energy sources and the dramatic increase in the energy demand, the need for the development of clean, efficient, and sustainable energy sources has never been greater. [(22)](javascript:void(0);) As a clean energy source, hydrogen has the advantages of high calorific value, environmental safety, and lack of secondary pollution. [(23)](javascript:void(0);) However, disadvantages such as expensive production and difficult storage limit its development. The traditional hydrogen synthesis methods pose potential environmental pollution risks, [(24)](javascript:void(0);) while photocatalytic water splitting is considered an efficient and eco-friendly strategy for hydrogen production. [(25)](javascript:void(0);) However, photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production still suffers from unfavorable thermodynamics, slow kinetics, dissolved oxygen, reverse reaction, and secondary reaction issues, which need to be overcome. [(26)](javascript:void(0);) It has been reported that plasmonic materials can be used as photocatalysts to effectively overcome these problems, based on the broadband absorption of sunlight and self-assembled structures of nanoparticles (NPs). [(27−35)](javascript:void(0);) Herein, we focus on new strategies for photocatalytic water splitting based on plasmonic materials developed in recent years.

### Composite Photocatalysts Based on Plasmonic Materials

Semiconductor photocatalysts have the advantages of low cost and high efficiency for continuous water splitting. However, their photocatalytic activity remains low, owing to their poor light absorption and fast recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and overcoming these issues remains a challenge. [(36)](javascript:void(0);) In recent years, the development of plasmonic heterostructures has emerged as a promising strategy to address these limitations, because it can effectively broaden the light absorption range of photocatalysts and suppress charge carrier recombination.

Some studies have been recently carried out to enhance the water decomposition ability of photocatalysts. Xu et al. reported a g-C3N4/TiN (denoted as CN/TiN) plasmonic nanocomposite photocatalyst. [(37)](javascript:void(0);) CN/TiN efficiently combined semiconductors and plasmonic metals by acting as a light-absorbing antenna and hot electron donor for TiN, thus enhancing water splitting in the visible and near-infrared light regions. This nanoheterojunction structure achieved a maximum hydrogen yield rate of 1308.80 μmol·h–1·g–1 through a plasma-induced hot electron transfer (PHET) mechanism.

As a wide-bandgap semiconductor, TiO2 has light response only in the ultraviolet region, where the solar spectrum coverage is less than 5%. Previous studies have shown that enhancing the visible light absorption of photoelectrodes by using the localized surface plasmon resonance effect generated by metal NPs can be effective in overcoming this issue. [(38,39)](javascript:void(0);) For example, Sang et al. reported an Au-reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/TiO2 photoelectrode. [(40)](javascript:void(0);) TiO2 provided a transport channel for photogenerated electrons, while the Au NPs endowed Au-rGO/TiO2 with a localized surface plasmon resonance effect, and Au and rGO synergistically enhanced heat transfer. The results showed that the hydrogen evolution rate of Au-rGO/TiO2 could reach 431.41 μL·h–1·cm–2.

To tune the light trapping ability of plasmonic metals for enhancing the hydrogen yield, Wu et al. recently reported a new strategy to improve the light trapping ability of Au NPs by a vertically ⟨001⟩-oriented anatase mesocrystal (meso-TiO2) grown on Ti foil as support. [(41)](javascript:void(0);) This system improved the multireflection of incident light between nanoparticles, as well as the light trapping properties of Au NPs. Consequently, the strength of the electromagnetic field surrounding the Au NPs was increased, boosting their catalytic activity. The experimental results confirmed that this strategy could achieve a hydrogen yield of 518.46 μL·cm–2. Choi et al. reported a new strategy for defect repair-mediated preparation of heterojunctions for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production. [(42)](javascript:void(0);) Oxygen vacancy (Vo) defects in ZnO provided active sites for the diffusion of individual Au atoms. Electron repair of surface defects by individual Au atoms contributed to the formation of heterojunctions between ZnO and plasma Au NPs ([Figure 2](#fig2)a). Therefore, the Au/Vo-Au:ZnO-4 heterojunction extended the photoelectron lifetime and increased the donor charge density. The results showed that the output rate of hydrogen was 48.58 times higher than that of Vo-ZnO.

Doping with plasmonic metal nanomaterials can enhance the visible light response of semiconductor photocatalysts through localized surface plasmon resonance. [(38−42)](javascript:void(0);) However, the surfactant molecules covering plasmonic metal nanomaterials can potentially block the active sites or inhibit the surface charge transfer. In this regard, Ye et al. reported a strategy to assemble Au NPs into Au colloidosomes (AuCSs) without modifying their surfaces with surfactants. They further synthesized a core–shell composite Au–CdS photocatalyst (AuCS@CdS) to realize efficient hydrogen production. [(43)](javascript:void(0);) AuCS@CdS could effectively precipitate hydrogen due to its strong localized surface plasmon resonance and efficient photoelectron transport mechanism ([Figure 2](#fig2)b). Thus, under simulated sunshine irradiation, AuCS@CdS exhibited a high hydrogen evolution activity of 235.8 mmol·g–1·h–1.

Wu et al. reported the synergistic and efficient hydrogen production by Au NPs and MoS2 nanosheets in the visible spectral region. [(44)](javascript:void(0);) They showed that the synergistic photocatalytic performance of Au and MoS2 was related to the localized surface plasmon resonance wavelength of Au. The energy absorbed through the excitation of surface plasmons by Au nanocages was transferred to MoS2 nanosheets to promote electron–hole excitation in MoS2. As a result, the photocatalytic rate of Au–MoS2 was almost 40 times higher than that of MoS2. The localized surface plasmon resonance wavelength based on Au NPs closely matched the absorption edge of MoS2 nanosheets, and the photon energy could be efficiently used for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) through the synergistic interaction between the localized surface plasmon resonance effect and the favorable phase structure of MoS2 ([Figure 2](#fig2)c).

### Photoelectrical Hydrogen Production

Photoelectrical hydrogen production technology demonstrates groundbreaking synergistic advantages over other hydrogen generation strategies, owing to its unique trinity of “light–electricity–chemical energy” conversion mechanisms. [(45)](javascript:void(0);) Compared to the high energy consumption of conventional water electrolysis and the efficiency limitations of photocatalytic hydrogen production, this technology achieves directional separation of photogenerated carriers through gradient electric fields generated on semiconductor material surfaces under solar illumination. Thus, solar energy is directly converted into electrochemical potential energy which drives hydrogen evolution reactions. [(46,47)](javascript:void(0);)

In semiconductor compounds doped with metallic elements, the incorporation of metal nanoparticles facilitates charge rectification, thereby enabling efficient charge transfer processes. Leveraging this property, such semiconductors are frequently employed as photocatalysts or photoelectrodes in clean energy generation applications. [(48)](javascript:void(0);) Juárez-Ramírez et al. demonstrated the hydrogen production capabilities of ZnO and ZnO/Ag composite thin films synthesized by sol–gel method. [(49)](javascript:void(0);) The presence of silver nanoparticles promoted active site availability and enhanced charge separation, effectively suppressing electron–hole recombination in ZnO films. Notably, films containing 5% silver dopant exhibited approximately 3.5 times greater hydrogen evolution compared to undoped ZnO counterparts.

The efficient generation of hydrogen through photoelectrochemical water splitting relies on the effective transfer of light-induced electrons from light-harvesting materials to electrocatalysts. A notable advancement in this field was reported by Wang et al., who engineered plasmon-enhanced photoelectrical nanostructures by integrating monolayer molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) with Cu1.75S–Au hybrid nanoparticles for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). [(50)](javascript:void(0);) These plasmonic heterostructures exhibited exceptional HER enhancement, achieving a 29.5-fold increase in photocurrent under 650 nm excitation. Efficient charge separation and abundant active edge sites enhanced the HER performance, benefiting from the broad light absorption (400–3000 nm) of the monolayer MoS.

### Bimetallic Structures for Enhanced Plasmonic Heating

Precious metals such as gold can effectively confine and modulate light in the subwavelength range owing to their excellent optical properties and HER activity, and thus are widely used as cocatalysts. [(51)](javascript:void(0);) However, their high cost and scarcity limit their industrial-scale application as photocatalysts. To overcome this challenge, the formation of bimetallic structures by doping earth-abundant metals has been proposed as a potential strategy for efficient photocatalysis. [(52)](javascript:void(0);)

Li et al. reported the synergistic enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production by Au NPs and cobalt porphyrin. [(53)](javascript:void(0);) CoTPyP molecules could be easily adsorbed on the surface of Au NPs to form an organic–inorganic hybrid nanostructure (AuNP@CoTPyP). The resulting material could efficiently generate hot carriers and transport them to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), resulting in strong HER reactivity ([Figure 2](#fig2)d). The hydrogen precipitation rate and turnover frequency of AuNP@CoTPyP reached 3.21 mol·g–1·h–1 and 4650 h–1, respectively, and the system remained stable after 45 h of catalytic cycling.

Recently, Zhang and Xue reported bimetallic Au and Ni nanostructures for the synergistic enhancement of the HER. [(54)](javascript:void(0);) The metallic Ni portion of AuNi could act as an additional electron sink and receive photogenerated electrons from the excited graphitic carbon nitride, promoting charge separation and providing more active surface sites for H+ adsorption and reduction to H2 ([Figure 2](#fig2)e). The HER activity of bimetallic structures loaded on graphitic carbon nitride far exceeded that of monometallic ones. Therefore, bimetallic nanostructures provide a low-cost and efficient strategy for the HER.

### Plasmonic Hydrogen Sensing

The increasing demand for hydrogen energy is stimulating the development of methods for its preparation and storage. However, hydrogen is flammable and explosive in nature, which emphasizes the importance of developing sensors for its accurate and stable detection. In recent years, a special optical hydrogen sensor based on the localized surface plasmon resonance of metallic nanostructures has shown excellent performance. [(55,56)](javascript:void(0);) Chen et al. reported Pd–Au nanostructures for plasmonic sensors. [(57)](javascript:void(0);) The Pd–Au sensor could eliminate the hysteresis between the hydrogen adsorption and desorption curves, resulting in an accurate hydrogen concentration signal. The nanoarray structure of the Pd–Au alloy improved the response/recovery kinetics. Moreover, the plasmonic hydrogen sensor was coated with a protective poly(methyl methacrylate) layer in order to maintain satisfactory performance in the presence of interfering gases.

Metal helicoid NPs with 3D chiral structure have considerable potential in chiral plasmonic sensing. Niu et al. reported Au@Pd helicoid NPs for integrated hydrogen sensors. [(58)](javascript:void(0);) Au@Pd could achieve a significant circular dichroism red shift up to 206.1 nm when exposed to hydrogen, and exhibited a good linear range over the hydrogen concentration range of 1.5–6.0%. This Au@Pd signaling sensor was obtained by integrating palladium shells on helicoid Au NPs.

## Solar-Driven Interfacial Desalination

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

Self-floating and highly light-absorbing materials generate localized thermal effects that can drive interfacial water generation even if the boiling point of water is not reached; [(59)](javascript:void(0);) this solar-driven interfacial desalination (SDID) technology is gradually being recognized and applied in research. A high light-to-heat conversion efficiency, good water recycling capability, and high salt resistance are the properties needed to achieve continuous, efficient, and stable evaporation. [(60)](javascript:void(0);) A diverse array of material types and structural design strategies have been employed for SDID, such as plasmonic materials, hydrogels, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), aerogels, and others. [(61−63)](javascript:void(0);) Plasmonic materials have a strong absorption capacity for light, due to the collective oscillation of local conduction electrons, causing the temperature of the particle surface to rise sharply, thus generating localized heat. [(64,65)](javascript:void(0);) Therefore, plasmonic materials enable the generation of steam at the liquid–vapor interface. In the following sections, we highlight some new concepts proposed for the structural design of SDID materials in recent years.

### Enhancement of Localized Heat Generation by Plasmonic Nanoparticles

Doped metal NPs can modulate the solar spectrum absorption, thereby enhancing localized thermal effects to enable the production of large amounts of steam from the evaporation of interfacial water. [(66)](javascript:void(0);) Evaporators loaded with plasmonic NPs enable rapid steam generation based on the fast water flow characteristics of nano/micropores. [(67)](javascript:void(0);) Bae et al. prepared flexible and thin black Au films by self-aggregation of collapsed nanowires to obtain different metal nanoscale gaps (0–200 nm) and microscale funnel structures ([Figure 3](#fig3)a). [(68)](javascript:void(0);) The resulting material achieved light absorption of 91% in the 400–2500 nm band, and a photothermal conversion efficiency of 57% at 20 kW·m–2. The micropores formed in the plasmonic membranes could enhance the SDID process, which promoted the continuous transport of interfacial water to the evaporating surface and enabled fast vapor removal due to localized heat. He et al. reported the formation of a large number of dense micropores on the surface of plasmonic membranes composed of Au NPs ([Figure 3](#fig3)b). [(69)](javascript:void(0);) This array of micropores also improved water delivery and vapor escape, resulting in continuous and constant evaporation. [(70)](javascript:void(0);)

Although the nano/microstructured plasmonic materials greatly enhance the light absorption of the evaporator in the solar spectral band, [(71)](javascript:void(0);) achieving a stable, sustained, and efficient SDID requires optimizing the balance between water transport, photothermal conversion, and salt elimination, and avoiding salt crystallization and deposition during the evaporation process. [(72)](javascript:void(0);) Jiang et al. reported that plasmonic Bi NPs in a porous carbon framework (Bi-C) achieved sustained and efficient water evaporation in high-salinity brine. [(73)](javascript:void(0);) The comparison of the surface nano/microstructures of Bi-C/CF

enriched_points.expanded_content: Plasmonic nanostructures significantly enhance solar energy efficiency through improved light absorption and charge separation, vital for solar cells and photocatalytic systems.

Recent studies demonstrate that integrating plasmonic and photonic nanostructures optimizes energy conversion processes, leading to increased efficiencies in photovoltaic and photothermal applications.

By enabling efficient solar energy conversion and clean hydrogen production, plasmonic nanostructures support global sustainable development goals, addressing energy crises and environmental concerns.

The ongoing research in plasmonic nanostructures promises innovative solutions for energy applications, including enhanced solar desalination, hydrogen production, and wastewater purification technologies.
